topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My fingers, clumsy and numb, hovered over the screen before finally clicking to open the subject line: "Update Regarding Sarah's Celebration." A cold knot tightened in my stomach, rapidly constricting my chest. I reread the body of the message three times. The words confirmed what I already suspected: the surprise party was off. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I stared at the screen, the pixels blurring into an unreadable mess. A strange, hollow sensation washed over me, making the room spin slightly. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room, my movements jerky and uncontrolled. Every sound in the house—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant traffic—seemed amplified, each noise a sharp intrusion. I needed to check my phone, to call someone, anything to ground myself, but my limbs felt heavy, unresponsive.

[story 2]

I walked into the kitchen, the silence of the apartment pressing in on me. The notification glowed on my tablet, mocking me with its finality. Cancelled. The word echoed in the sudden quiet, loud and sharp in the enclosed space. My hands began to shake so violently I had to grip the counter edge to steady them. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drum solo demanding attention. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the image of the planned celebration—the laughter, the decorations, everyone smiling—back into my mind, but it dissolved into a sickening void. I took a ragged, deep breath, but it caught in my throat, turning into a dry, painful rasp. I started pacing the length of the floor, my gaze darting towards the window as if expecting someone to materialize there, judging the silence.

[story 3]

When I finally read the confirmation, the air seemed to evacuate the room. It was a slow, creeping paralysis that started at my fingertips and radiated outward. My vision tunneled slightly; the edges of the furniture seemed to shift, becoming indistinct shapes. I dropped the tablet onto the sofa cushion with a soft thud that sounded deafeningly loud. I couldn't move. My legs felt like lead weights, refusing to obey any command. A strange, hot flush crept up my neck, mingling with a deep, unsettling chill that seemed to originate from my core. I pressed my palms against my eyes, trying to physically block out the reality of the situation, but the sight of the cancelled plans was too vivid, too stark. A low, involuntary sound escaped my lips—a small, choked gasp that quickly morphed into a silent, internal tremor.
